sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I can see the pattern clearly:
- Player at row 14
- Red block at row 14, col 43
- Blue block at row 20, col 19 (moved from col 43 to col 19)
- Green block at row 26, col 19 (still at col 19)
So when the player moves up, the blocks that were at col 43 move to col 19. The red block is now at the same row as the player (row 14), so it's at col 43 (the target position). The blue and green blocks are at col 19 (the "resting" position).
This confirms the pattern:
1. The player moves up/down to position the track at different heights.
2. The colored block at the player's row is at col 43 (the target position).
3. The other colored blocks are at col 19 (the resting position).
4. The track extends from the player to the right, and when it reaches col 43, it connects to the colored block at that height.
Now I need to extend the track to connect to the red block. Let me press RIGHT to extend the track.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Player is at row 14, red block is at col 43. Extend track to connect to red block.
print("Extending track to connect to red block...")
action(['RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT'])

# Check state after extending
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("\n=== Colored blocks after extending ===")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['R', 'b', 'N', 'M'] and n['pixels'] >= 16:
        center_r = sum(p[0] for p in n['boundary']) // len(n['boundary'])
        center_c = sum(p[1] for p in n['boundary']) // len(n['boundary'])
        print(f"  {n['color']}: pixels={n['pixels']} center=({center_r}, {center_c})")

# Check bottom panel
current_ascii = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("\n=== Bottom panel ===")
for r in range(56, 62):
    print(f"Row {r}: {current_ascii[r][18:45]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
